rationale: `CryptoCard.tsx` declares `interface CryptoCardProps { coin: CryptoCurrency }`. `CryptoGrid.tsx` calls `<CryptoCard crypto={c} />` — passing a prop named `crypto`, not `coin`. At runtime the component receives `coin` as undefined and crashes on `coin.price.toFixed()`. `react_prop_consistency` walks every JSX call site: extracts the component's declared Props interface, parses the attribute names in the open tag, subtracts known builtins (key, ref, className, on*, data-*, aria-*), and flags any remaining attributes that aren't in the declared Props. The case has no `tsconfig.json`, so `tsc` skips cleanly — this layer is the only check that catches the mismatch. Even with tsconfig, tsc would catch it later (TS2322), but with a noisier signal buried in compiler output. The static layer surfaces it as a clean per-component report.

baseline_comparison.tsc: WOULD CATCH — TS2322 if tsconfig is present and the file compiles. Often deferred in agent-generated code until a build runs.
baseline_comparison.eslint: PASS — vanilla eslint doesn't track prop types across components.
baseline_comparison.eslint-plugin-react: PARTIAL — react/prop-types runs against PropTypes (PropTypes-only); doesn't validate TypeScript interface usage.
baseline_comparison.raw_llm_critique: UNRELIABLE — may or may not connect the consumer's prop name to the component's declared interface.
baseline_comparison.manual_QA: Catches when the component first renders and crashes.
